name: Mathlib
note: The Lean community's mathematical library. Every declaration in it is already machine-checked by the same kernel this registry uses, so the registry treats all of it as done. New holes should state what Mathlib does not yet contain; a hole later found to duplicate a Mathlib result is closed by citation, not re-proved.

name: formal-conjectures (Google DeepMind)
note: A collection of conjecture statements formalized in Lean, centered on the problems of Paul Erdos as catalogued at erdosproblems.com. This is the closest existing thing to a frontier registry: precise Lean statements of problems nobody has solved. Holes here should link to the corresponding formal-conjectures entry rather than restate it.
stats: open problems in the formalization milestone

name: Physlib (formerly PhysLean / HepLean)
note: An open-source community project to digitalize results from physics into Lean 4 - definitions, theorems, and calculations, with Wick's theorem as a documented example. Physics formalization is far earlier-stage than mathematics: most of the subject has no formal counterpart yet, which makes it the widest open frontier reachable from Lean today.

title: Fermat's Last Theorem, formalized
body: The FLT project led by Kevin Buzzard at Imperial College London is formalizing Fermat's Last Theorem in Lean, with the interim goal of reducing it to results that were known to experts by 1989. It is the largest single formalization effort in mathematics and is not in Mathlib. Reference: https://github.com/ImperialCollegeLondon/FLT. Holes under this proposal should track the project's stated open sub-goals rather than the full theorem.
author: registry

title: Erdos-Straus conjecture
body: For every integer n >= 2, the fraction 4/n can be written as 1/x + 1/y + 1/z with x, y, z positive integers. Open since 1948. A Lean statement is straightforward to pin, which makes this a good first hole to formalize from this list. See erdosproblems.com and the formal-conjectures corpus for the statement conventions to follow.
author: registry

title: Erdos-Turan conjecture on additive bases
body: If A is a set of natural numbers such that every sufficiently large integer is a sum of two elements of A, then the number of ways to write n as such a sum cannot stay bounded. Open since 1941. Like PRP-002, the statement is elementary enough to formalize directly against core Lean or Mathlib.
author: registry

title: Noether's theorem for classical field theories
body: Physics frontier. Physlib (the corpus above) is digitalizing physics into Lean, and most of classical mechanics and field theory has no formal counterpart. A machine-checked derivation of Noether's theorem - every differentiable symmetry of the action corresponds to a conservation law - would be a foundational entry that many later results depend on. First task: pin what 'action', 'symmetry', and 'conserved current' mean formally.
author: registry

title: The Ito integral for Mathlib
body: Missing Mathlib library. Mathlib's probability theory does not yet reach stochastic calculus: the Ito integral, Ito's formula, and stochastic differential equations have no usable formal development. This blocks formal work on mathematical finance, filtering, and stochastic PDEs. The hole structure should follow the decomposition the informal theory already has: predictable processes, the isometry, the integral, then the formula.
author: registry

title: The heat equation and its smoothing estimates
body: Missing Mathlib library. Mathlib has strong foundations in measure theory and functional analysis but very little partial differential equation theory. The heat equation on R^n - existence of solutions via the heat kernel, uniqueness under growth conditions, smoothing - is the standard first target, and its absence blocks most of formal mathematical physics.
author: registry

name: 1000+ theorems list
note: A community catalogue of notable theorems and their formalization status across proof assistants, maintained as the successor of Freek Wiedijk's 'Formalizing 100 Theorems'. The 975 entries without a Lean formalization are ingested below as proposals: each needs a pinned statement, written with Mathlib's definitions, before it is a solvable hole, and that formalization work is itself attributed on this log.

title: Noether's second theorem
body: From the 1000+ theorems list, a community catalogue of notable theorems and which proof assistants have formalized them. As of 2026-07-03 this entry records no Lean formalization. Mathematics Subject Classification: 49. Background: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Noether's_second_theorem. Source: https://1000-plus.github.io/ (entry Q10369454). A hole under this proposal should pin a Lean statement in the Mathlib environment (razor hole --env mathlib), using Mathlib's definitions so an admitted proof is ready to build on.

body: From the 1000+ theorems list, a community catalogue of notable theorems and which proof assistants have formalized them. As of 2026-07-03 this entry records no Lean formalization. Mathematics Subject Classification: 53. Background: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Four-vertex_theorem. Source: https://1000-plus.github.io/ (entry Q994401). A hole under this proposal should pin a Lean statement in the Mathlib environment (razor hole --env mathlib), using Mathlib's definitions so an admitted proof is ready to build on.

author: registry
registry
notes: core Lean, no dependencies; see lean/Razor/Frontier/ErdosStraus.lean
gloss: for every n >= 2 there are positive x, y, z with 4xyz = n(yz + xz + xy) - the fraction form 4/n = 1/x + 1/y + 1/z with denominators cleared, so it needs no rational-number library

author: registry
notes: Mathlib environment (lean-mathlib/); fetch it with ./mathlib-env.sh. Proving this equivalent to STM-ES-CORE is an open convergence task.
gloss: for every n >= 2 there are positive integers x, y, z with 4/n = 1/x + 1/y + 1/z, stated directly over Mathlib's rationals

author: registry
notes: Mathlib environment (lean-mathlib/RazorMathlib/Statements.lean), stated with Set.ncard and Filter.atTop
gloss: if every sufficiently large natural number is a sum of two elements of A, the number of representations n = a + b with a, b in A is unbounded; ordered pairs are counted, which changes nothing about boundedness

title: Erdos-Turan on additive bases (in the Mathlib environment)

author: registry
notes: core Lean, no dependencies; see lean/Razor/Frontier/FLT.lean. The FLT project states the theorem using Mathlib's definitions; proving the two forms equivalent is an open convergence task.
gloss: for every exponent n >= 3 there are no positive natural numbers x, y, z with x^n + y^n = z^n

notes: the near-miss 3^3 + 4^3 = 91 is rejected at exponent 3 (not 125)

author: registry
notes: the Prop is defined by Mathlib itself (FermatLastTheorem, in Mathlib.NumberTheory.FLT.Basic) - the exact statement the Imperial FLT project is proving. Proving it equivalent to STM-FLT-CORE is an open convergence task.
gloss: Mathlib's own statement: for every n >= 3 there are no positive naturals x, y, z with x^n + y^n = z^n
